source.name: Sources Catalog
source.publisher: CA Dollar Trace (derived from the cited source configs)
source.license: Derived work; each underlying source keeps its own license

cadence: regenerated each run from the source configs
caveats: This is a directory of the machine-readable sources the site ingests; each source's own coverage and caveats are shown with it.

sources.name: California Enacted Budget 2025-26 (ebudget)
sources.cadence: budget cycle (enacted June/July; revised at Governor's Budget & May Revision)
caveats: General Fund view: enacted-budget figures (budgetary/legal basis), not actual cash spent; actuals arrive via Open FI$Cal with a ~60-day lag and will not reconcile exactly.
GF expenditures ($228.4B) exceed GF revenues ($215.7B) in the enacted plan; the difference is covered by reserves/carryover, and we show that gap rather than hiding it.
Agency all-funds totals include federal funds and reimbursements passing through the state budget.
sources.feeds: The enacted state budget — what the Legislature appropriated, by fund and program area.

sources.name: California Enacted Budget 2025-26 — Department Detail (ebudget)
sources.cadence: budget cycle (enacted June/July); re-polled with the summary source
caveats: Enacted-budget program and fund detail (budgetary/legal basis), not actual cash spent.
Department program lines are validated to sum to each department's published total; departments where they do not are flagged, not silently dropped.
Fund classes: G=General Fund, S=special, F=federal, B=bond. Federal funds are federal money administered through the state budget.
sources.feeds: Department- and program-level detail beneath each agency's budget.

sources.name: Open FI$Cal — Department Vendor Transactions
sources.cadence: monthly, ~60-day accounting lag
caveats: Vendor Transactions cover ~79% of state budgetary expenditures and EXCLUDE payroll, most social-service benefit payments (e.g. Medi-Cal benefits), purchase-card, and interfaced non-FI$Cal departments — so checkbook totals are far below budget totals by design; we publish the coverage percentage per department.

Amounts are NET of reversal/adjustment entries, which FI$Cal publishes deliberately (see its Learning Center on 'Negative Expenditures' and account 5390950 'SCO Inbound Interface'). Where a vendor's adjustments are material we also publish gross payments recorded, and we surface each department's net SCO-interface effect rather than hiding it.
Statutorily confidential vendors appear as 'Confidential'; we count and publish that share rather than dropping it.
Amounts are modified-accrual accounting transactions; they will not reconcile exactly to enacted budget figures.
sources.feeds: The state's checkbook — actual vendor payments, name by name.

sources.name: California Grants Portal
sources.cadence: daily, published ~8:45pm PT
caveats: Awardee-level at best; the portal collects no subrecipient detail (only a flag that subrecipients exist).
EstAvailFunds is self-reported by grantmakers and may be blank; blanks are reported as 'amount unknown', never zero.
sources.feeds: State grant programs open for application, refreshed nightly.

sources.name: California Grants Portal — Grant Awards
sources.cadence: daily, published ~8:45pm PT
caveats: Post-award data exists only for grants closing on/after July 1, 2022 (AB 132) — earlier grant awards are not published here.
The portal records only WHETHER subrecipients exist per award, never who they are — every award marked 'has subrecipients' is a flagged dead end.
Award amounts are as reported by the granting agency; blanks are counted as unknown, never zero.
sources.feeds: The organizations awarded state grants, and for how much.

sources.name: BHCIP Awardees (Rounds 1-5 + Bond Rounds 1-2)
sources.publisher: DHCS / Advocates for Human Potential (program administrator)
sources.cadence: manual re-capture when new BHCIP rounds are announced
caveats: Awardee names, projects, and rounds only — per-project dollar amounts are published by DHCS/AHP in round announcements (PDF/web), not yet machine-readable here.
Snapshot captured from AHP's public Tableau dashboard 2026-07-16; it reflects grantee self-reported project data.
BHCIP money reaches these entities through AHP, a private third-party administrator — this hop exists in no state accounting dataset, which is why we recovered it from the program's own reporting.
sources.feeds: Behavioral-health infrastructure grants and the projects they funded.

sources.name: Government Compensation in California (2024)
sources.cadence: annual (SCO publishes the prior calendar year each fall)
caveats: Government Compensation in California, reported to the State Controller. Each row is one position's actual pay and benefits for the year, with the job title — the SCO strips employee names before publishing.
Covers state departments, counties, cities, K-12 school districts, community college districts, CSU, UC, special districts, and superior courts for 2024.
Pay shown is actual wages received in the calendar year — someone hired mid-year shows a partial year, which pulls medians down.

Not every government files: several large school districts (San Diego, San Francisco, Elk Grove, and Oakland Unified among them) reported no 2024 payroll to the Controller. A district that filed a prior year is shown with that year labeled; a district that never filed shows no payroll at all — absence is never invented.
Benefits shown are the employer's retirement + health/dental/vision contribution; pension unfunded-liability payments are reported separately by the systems.
sources.feeds: Government payroll — positions, wages, and benefits reported to the State Controller.

sources.name: Medi-Cal Managed Care — Enrollment & Capitation Rates
sources.cadence: enrollment monthly; capitation rate certifications per rating period
caveats: Enrollment counts are per plan per county per month as reported by DHCS; a plan's statewide figure sums its county rows.
Capitation rates are DHCS-certified RANGES (lower/midpoint/upper, per member per month) by rating period, county, and category of aid — they are prices, not amounts spent.
What each plan pays hospitals, clinics, and physicians remains non-public — this dataset extends visibility exactly one hop and no further.
Dental managed care (e.g. Liberty Dental) rates are certified separately and are not in these files; such plans show enrollment without a rate range.

Enrollment and rate datasets never agree on plan names; we join them with a published normalization + curated alias list and report the measured match rate (enrollee-weighted) instead of pretending it's complete.
sources.feeds: Medi-Cal managed-care enrollment and the certified rates paid per member.

sources.name: HCAI Hospital Annual Financial Data (Selected)
sources.publisher: CA Department of Health Care Access and Information
sources.cadence: annual filings; HCAI re-extracts ~twice a year as audits land
caveats: Each figure is the hospital's own annual disclosure to HCAI (Hospital Annual Financial Disclosure Report). The prior fiscal year is mostly audited; the latest year is largely still in process — every hospital carries its report status.

Medi-Cal figures are net revenue the hospital reports receiving for Medi-Cal patients (traditional fee-for-service and managed care separately) — a yearly total per hospital, not plan-by-plan payments.
Hospitals only: Medi-Cal money flowing to physicians, clinics, pharmacies, and other non-hospital providers is not in this data.
sources.feeds: Every hospital's own annual financials — including what it reports receiving from Medi-Cal.

sources.name: SCO ByTheNumbers — County Expenditures
sources.cadence: annual, rolling as late filers report (~18-month lag typical)
caveats: Self-reported by counties and 'posted as submitted' — the State Controller does not audit or restate these figures.
Category level only: there is no vendor detail here, and most counties publish no vendor-level checkbook anywhere.
Fiscal years appear as the ending year (2024 = FY2023-24); late-filing counties can be a year behind their peers.
57 counties appear here, not 58: the City and County of San Francisco reports through the cities dataset instead.
sources.feeds: County finances by category, self-reported to the State Controller.

sources.name: SCO ByTheNumbers — City Expenditures
sources.cadence: annual, rolling as late filers report (~18-month lag typical)
caveats: Self-reported by cities and 'posted as submitted' — the State Controller does not audit or restate these figures.
Category level only; a handful of cities (LA, SF, San Diego) publish real vendor checkbooks elsewhere — most publish nothing deeper than this.
Detail is published for the largest cities by spending; the remainder is disclosed as an aggregate, with every city in the raw download.
sources.feeds: City finances by category, self-reported to the State Controller.

sources.name: SCO ByTheNumbers — Special District Expenditures
sources.cadence: annual, rolling as late filers report
caveats: Self-reported by districts and 'posted as submitted' — no vendor detail exists, and no special district publishes a checkbook.
Independent special districts are the layer of California government residents know least; JPAs report even less consistently.
Detail is published for the largest districts by spending; the remainder is disclosed as an aggregate, with every district in the raw download.
sources.feeds: Special-district finances by category, self-reported to the State Controller.

sources.name: SACS Unaudited Actuals — K-12 District Finances
sources.cadence: annual (unaudited actuals publish ~8-9 months after FY end)
caveats: Unaudited actuals, self-reported by ~2,000 local educational agencies; audited figures can differ.

SACS accounts categorize spending (salaries, benefits, services, capital) — vendor names do not exist anywhere in this system; who a district actually paid requires district-by-district records requests.
Amounts are decoded from SACS account strings by fund and object code; the full account detail is preserved in the raw archive.
sources.feeds: K-12 school-district spending, self-reported unaudited actuals.

sources.name: CDE Principal Apportionment Summary
sources.cadence: certified ~3x/year (Advance Jul, P-1 Feb, P-2 Jun); URL pinned per certification
caveats: This is the STATE-AID portion of school funding — the Principal Apportionment the state pays through counties. Local property taxes that fund the rest of each district's LCFF entitlement are not in these figures.
Figures are the certified apportionment calculation for the fiscal year shown, including prior-year adjustments; monthly cash follows the State Controller's payment schedule.
The apportionment year is the CURRENT budget year, while district spending (SACS) reports the last completed year — the two sit side by side and are never subtracted from each other.
sources.feeds: What the state certified it would pay each school district — the sending side of K-12 money.

sources.name: City Vendor Checkbooks — Los Angeles & San Francisco
sources.cadence: LA: continuous (transactions); SF: weekly contract-system refresh
caveats: Los Angeles figures are actual payment transactions for the most recent COMPLETE city fiscal year (LA's year ends June 30). Large financial-institution payees (custodial banks, bond trustees) reflect money movement, not purchases.
San Francisco figures are cumulative payments to date on contracts in the city's current procurement system — a different measure than annual spending; treat the two cities' numbers as incomparable.
These two checkbooks exist because each city chose to publish one — most of California's cities and counties publish nothing like this.
sources.feeds: Transaction-level checkbooks for the cities that publish one (LA & SF).

sources.name: Funding Positions of CalPERS Contracting Agencies
sources.cadence: annual (actuarial valuations, published ~a year after each June 30)
caveats: Covers CalPERS contracting agencies with their own actuarial plans. Small agencies pooled into CalPERS risk pools are reported only in aggregate elsewhere, and governments with independent retirement systems — Los Angeles (city and county), San Francisco, and about 20 county systems — are not in CalPERS data at all.
Unfunded liability is the gap between what's been promised and what's been set aside, at the actuary's assumptions; it is a debt paid down through future budgets.
sources.feeds: Each CalPERS local government's pension debt — promised vs. set aside.

sources.name: USAspending — federal awards into California (FY2025)
sources.cadence: monthly-ish (agency submissions roll in continuously; subawards ~monthly)
caveats: Transaction obligations for awards performed in California, federal FY2025 (Oct 2024-Sep 2025); obligations are not the same as cash outlays.
'MULTIPLE RECIPIENTS' aggregates payments to individuals (e.g. direct benefits) that are masked for privacy — shown as masked, not omitted.
Subaward reporting below prime recipients is federally required only above $30,000 and is known to be incomplete.
sources.feeds: Federal awards flowing into California, with subaward hand-offs where reported.

sources.name: Federal Audit Clearinghouse — California Single Audits
sources.cadence: rolling (audits filed ~9 months after each entity's fiscal year end)
caveats: Covers entities that expended at least the single-audit threshold ($750k, raised to $1M for fiscal years starting Oct 2024) in federal awards — smaller recipients are not audited here.
Amounts are each entity's total federal expenditures per its audited SEFA, not payments between entities; documented federal-agency coding error rates apply.
The most recent audit year is always incomplete (audits arrive ~9 months after each entity's year ends); we publish the latest year with substantial coverage and disclose counts.
sources.feeds: Independently audited federal spending (single audits) — the one audited source.

sources.name: USAspending — Federal Subawards in California
sources.cadence: subawards reported ~monthly; compliance is federally acknowledged as incomplete
caveats: Subaward reporting is required only above $30,000 and compliance is notoriously incomplete — these are the reported edges, not all edges.
Amounts are subaward obligations for awards performed in California in the stated federal fiscal year.
A state agency appearing as prime recipient with a county as sub-awardee is the federal slice of the state-to-county pass-through — state-funded flows to the same counties are not in this dataset.
sources.feeds: Federal money re-granted to sub-recipients, one hop past the prime.

sources.name: CA AG Charity Registry + IRS 990 (via ProPublica)
sources.cadence: AG registry updated 1st & 3rd Wednesday monthly; 990 data lags 12-18 months
caveats: Matching is by normalized organization name — a published match rate, not a guarantee; unmatched entities may still be registered under a different legal name.
'May not operate' status means the AG registry lists the charity as delinquent, suspended, or revoked — it is a compliance flag, not a fraud finding.
990 figures lag 12-18 months behind reality and cover the organization's whole operation, not its California government revenue.
sources.feeds: Charity registration standing and IRS Form 990 filings for nonprofits.

sources.name: IRS Form 990 Part VII — Officers, Directors & Key Employees
sources.cadence: IRS posts bulk e-file XML monthly, ~a year behind filing; re-captured when new zips land
caveats: Names, titles, and compensation come from each organization's own IRS Form 990, Part VII — a public federal disclosure. Compensation is what the organization reported paying that person for the filing year shown.
Total shown is compensation from the organization plus related organizations plus estimated other compensation, as reported on the form.
The IRS releases e-filed returns on a delay, so the latest available filing is typically one to two years old; every figure carries its filing year.
An organization missing here either isn't in the IRS bulk XML yet or files a form without Part VII — absence is not a zero.
sources.feeds: Who runs each nonprofit and what they're paid — named officers from IRS Form 990.

derived.name: Cross-Source Entity Crosswalk
derived.feeds: One organization unified across every source above, so its full footprint reads as a single profile.

derived.name: Vendor Search Index
derived.feeds: The name-search index that jumps you straight to an organization's trail.
